medications.indication_rationale: Do not initiate rate-control drugs over telehealth before an ECG excludes arrhythmia and the diagnosis is confirmed; they can cause hypotension and worsen orthostatic symptoms. These are specialist/GP-initiated after assessment.

medications.drug_name: oral rehydration / increased fluid and salt intake
medications.indication_rationale: Increasing fluid and salt is first-line supportive therapy for POTS, but advise sensible amounts and check there is no renal/cardiac contraindication rather than 'aggressive high-dose' loading.
medications.dose_route_frequency: increased oral fluids and dietary salt within sensible limits
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no renal/cardiac contraindication before high-salt loading

medications.indication_rationale: No established evidence for these in post-COVID POTS; should not be presented as therapy.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Graded, recumbent-based exercise reconditioning is a cornerstone of POTS management.
Fall-safety, energy conservation and home aids (shower chair).

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Physical counter-manoeuvres (calf pumping, leg crossing before standing)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Mechanically improve venous return and reduce pre-syncope; safe and immediately useful.
Abdominal/lower-limb compression reduces venous pooling in POTS.
No evidence base for post-COVID POTS; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: With your parent, rise slowly in stages, do calf pumps before standing, and watch for true fainting, chest pain, or a racing heart that persists lying flat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces falls and safety-nets cardiac features.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase fluids and dietary salt sensibly (unless told otherwise), and drink a glass of fluid before getting up.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Expands blood volume and reduces postural symptoms.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Begin gentle recumbent/graded exercise as advised, and use a shower chair for bathing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reconditioning improves POTS while avoiding falls.

patient_education_points.point: When you stand, your blood vessels aren't tightening properly, so blood pools in your legs and your heart races to push it back up — that's why standing makes you dizzy and it settles when you lie down. This fits a post-viral condition called POTS.
We still want a heart tracing (ECG) in person to be sure the racing isn't coming from a heart rhythm problem, so please arrange a prompt in-person review.
Increasing fluids and salt and doing calf pumps before standing can help now — but we shouldn't start heart-slowing medications over a chat before you've been assessed.
Call 000 if you actually faint and hit the floor, get chest pain, or your heart keeps racing even after lying down for 20 minutes.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person GP review within a few days with an ECG; routine autonomic/cardiology referral for confirmation and management
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: ECG, postural vitals, hydration/salt plan, fall safety, exercise reconditioning, medication need
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: True syncope, chest pain, or tachycardia persisting lying flat — call 000.

must_include_items: Screen and safety-net arrhythmia/true syncope (rest palpitations, LOC, chest pain, tachycardia persisting supine)
Do NOT start beta-blockers/ivabradine over chat before ECG/diagnosis
Arrange prompt in-person review with an ECG and manage fall risk (supervision, shower chair, slow rising)
Provide supportive POTS measures (sensible fluids/salt, counter-manoeuvres, compression)
acceptable_alternatives: Supervised home active-stand test to document the postural HR rise
Same-day ED if any red-flag feature is present
errors_of_omission: Diagnosing POTS without any confirmation pathway
errors_of_commission: Recommending very aggressive salt loading without contraindication check
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) arrhythmia/syncope safety-netting with an ECG pathway, (2) no remote initiation of rate-control drugs, and (3) fall-safety plus sensible supportive measures. Prescribing beta-blockers on demand or ignoring fall risk is a fail.
